title: Operational assessment of a potentiometric eight-sensor flow cell in a portable flow injection analyzer
abstract:
Operational Assessment of a Potentiometric Eight-Sensor
Flow Cell in a Portable Flow Injection Analyzer
Peter W. Alexander* and Telis Dimitrakopoulos
Department of Physical Sciences, University of Tasmania, P.O. Box 1214, Launceston, Tasmania 7250, Australia
D. Brynn Hibbert
Department of Analytical Chemistry, University of New South Wales, Sydney 2052, Australia
The evaluation of a novel eight-sensor ﬂow cell used in
a portable battery-powered ﬂow injection potentiometry
system is described. The portable ﬂow injection system
is small in size, light weight, requires low power, and
uses a notebook computer for real-time display of data
and data acquisition, making it practical for remote site
monitoring. The electrode response for each sensor
was evaluated in terms of ﬂow rate and injection sam-
ple volume with silver and iodide standard solutions,
each in a 0.1-M sodium acetate background. The mean
electrode slopes observed for silver and iodide ions
were 57.4 6 1.0 and 47.5 6 4.4 mV per activity decade,
respectively.
Potential applications include multiion 
capability with eight different liquid-membrane-based
electrodes or operation of the measuring electrodes in
an additive mode for improved sensitivity and detection
limit for a single analyte. © 1996 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
Keywords: Portable instrumentation, Battery-powered,
Flow injection potentiometry, Sensor array
Introduction
There has been considerable work in the development of
multisensor systems in either ﬂowing streams, such as ﬂow
injection potentiometry (FIP)1 – 9 or steady-state10,11 mea-
surements. Employing potentiometric measurements in the
FIP mode is more advantageous than the steady-state mode,
in terms of fast sample throughput rate (.120 injections/h),
use of small sample volumes (#200 ml), and high preci-
sion. Moreover, electrode drift can be corrected when mea-
suring peak heights and computer-controlled automation of
a ﬂow injection system can easily be developed.12
Recently, the development of portable (battery-powered)
ﬂow injection systems has been reported with the use of a
commercial ion-selective electrode (ISE);13 a metallic wire
electrode;14 and a sodium, potassium, and nitrate planar de-
sign ISE array15 as detectors. These portable ﬂow injection
systems were designed to monitor samples at remote site
locations, where no main power source was available. The
present article concerns the evaluation of a ﬂow cell design
containing eight electrodes to be employed in a portable
battery-powered ﬂow injection system.
The need for ﬁeld-portable instrumentation based on
ﬂow injection analysis has been reviewed recently.16 There
are numerous types of electrochemical probes17,18 and some
continuous ﬂow and ﬂow injection analyzers based on col-
orimetric measurements reported for the analysis of aque-
ous samples.19,20 Other portable instrumentation, including
an anodic stripping voltammetry system21,22 for determining
trace metals, a remote electrode operated in the stripping
potentiometric mode to measure labile copper in seawater,23
and a remote electrochemical biosensor for monitoring phe-
nolic compounds in untreated river and groundwater sam-
ples,24 has been reported. Portability has also been achieved
in many other instrumental areas, including mass spectrom-
etry, gas chromatography, ion-mobility spectrometry, in-
frared and UV spectroscopy, x-ray ﬂuorescence spectrom-
etry, and laser radar spectrometry.25
There have been numerous reports on multielectrode
ﬂow cell designs using either ISEs or ion-selective ﬁeld-
effect transistors. However, no more than four sensors have
been used in an array for multiion determinations, and none
were applied in a portable ﬂow injection system. An array
of up to six identical ISEs was used to determine one ana-
lyte in the FIP mode by employing the cells-in-series ap-
proach.26 – 30 Advantages of employing cells-in-series with
identical electrodes were the improvements in sensitivity
and in detection limits compared to a single electrode sys-
tem. This approach has been used sucessfully for measure-
ments of cyanide,29 chloride,26 ﬂuoride,28 and nitrate30 ions.
FIELD ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY AND TECHNOLOGY. 1(1):31–37, 1996
CCC1086-900X/96/010031-07
*Corresponding author.
Received January 15, 1996; revised January 31, 1996; accepted February
5, 1996.
© 1996 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
 32
FIELD ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY AND TECHNOLOGY—1996
This article reports on the design and validation of a
portable battery-powered ﬂow injection system that em-
ploys a silver sensor octuplet ﬂow cell design. Although a
similar ﬂow cell design was presented in a previous study
containing four measuring electrodes, the ﬂow injection
manifold was a desktop system,4,9 and operation as a
portable system was not demonstrated. Electrode responses
to silver and iodide standard solutions were used to evalu-
ate the performance of the ﬂow cell design in terms of elec-
trode slope and log-linear range with various ﬂow rates and
sample volumes.
Experiment
Materials
All reagents were used as received from the suppliers:
silver nitrate (Aldrich), potassium iodide (BDH, AR) and
sodium acetate (BDH, AR). The silver and the iodide stan-
dard solutions were prepared by serial dilution from a stock
solution of 0.1-M silver nitrate and 0.1-M of potassium io-
dide. Ultrapure water (Barnstead Ultrapure water systems,
18.0 MV) was used throughout this study.
Design of the Portable Flow Injection System
The ﬂow injection system consisted of components de-
veloped in house and constructed in a carry case. A dia-
gram of the entire system is given in Figure 1. The carry
case consisted of a battery pack containing six Ni-Cd
rechargeable 1.2 volt batteries, a two-channel peristaltic
pump (Model OEM-S2) obtained from A. I. Scientiﬁc Pty.
Ltd. (Australia), a modiﬁed Rheodyne 5020 (controlled by
a small motor that can switch from the load to the inject po-
sition in 0.5 s via a switch located outside the case), and an
analog-to-digital converter described elsewhere.13 – 15 The
weight of the entire system was 2.1 kg.
Flow Injection Measurement Procedure
The portable FIP system described above was used at
ﬂow rates varying between 1.5 and 2.8 ml/min through two
channels, one for the reference electrode and the other for
the eight sensors. The ﬂow ratio between the carrier and the
reference streams of the multisensor ﬂow cell used was at
1:1. The silver and iodide standard solutions were intro-
duced manually to ﬁll the sample loop using the modiﬁed
Rheodyne (5020) injection valve described above, ﬁtted
with sample volumes ranging between 100 and 250 ml. The
carrier and reference streams used for all ﬂow injection
measurements was 100-mM sodium acetate, and in the case
of silver ion measurements 0.01-mM AgCl was added to
the 100-mM sodium acetate carrier. Prior to use, the eight
silver-based sensors were conditioned in a 100-mM sodium
acetate/0.1-mM silver iodide solution overnight.
The data acquired for each of the silver sensors were
displayed simultaneously in real-time millivolt readings on
a single computer window using the Satod© (Ver. 1.37)
data acquisition program, and were saved as text ﬁles. A
software package called IGOR PRO (AD Instruments, Syd-
ney) was used for data analysis and graphical representa-
tion of the data.
Results and Discussion
Design of the Multisensor Flow Cell
As shown in previous studies, the appropriate design of
a miniature multisensor ﬂow cell requires the wall-embed-
ded conﬁguration,12 whereby the sensor is positioned in the
ﬂow channel and the injected sample zone ﬂows over the
surface of the electrode. This arrangement allows several
sensors to be positioned in a single ﬂow stream, whereby a
single injected sample zone can contact each of the measur-
ing electrodes while maintaining limited dispersion. See
Figure 2.
The position of the reference electrode could be chosen
either downstream from the sensors or in a differential
arrangement, where the reference electrode is contained in
a separate ﬂow channel that merges downstream with the
multisensor channel. Studies have shown that the differen-
tial approach in potentiometric ﬂow-through cells leads to
FIG. 1.
Diagram of the portable battery-powered ﬂow injection analyzer.

Peter W. Alexander* and Telis Dimitrakopoulos

Department of Analytical Chemistry, University of New South Wales, Sydney 2052, Australia

uses a notebook computer for real-time display of data
and data acquisition, making it practical for remote site
monitoring. The electrode response for each sensor
was evaluated in terms of ﬂow rate and injection sam-
ple volume with silver and iodide standard solutions,
each in a 0.1-M sodium acetate background. The mean
electrode slopes observed for silver and iodide ions
were 57.4 6 1.0 and 47.5 6 4.4 mV per activity decade,

All reagents were used as received from the suppliers:
silver nitrate (Aldrich), potassium iodide (BDH, AR) and
sodium acetate (BDH, AR). The silver and the iodide stan-
dard solutions were prepared by serial dilution from a stock
solution of 0.1-M silver nitrate and 0.1-M of potassium io-
dide. Ultrapure water (Barnstead Ultrapure water systems,
18.0 MV) was used throughout this study.
Design of the Portable Flow Injection System
The ﬂow injection system consisted of components de-
veloped in house and constructed in a carry case. A dia-
gram of the entire system is given in Figure 1. The carry
case consisted of a battery pack containing six Ni-Cd
rechargeable 1.2 volt batteries, a two-channel peristaltic
pump (Model OEM-S2) obtained from A. I. Scientiﬁc Pty.
Ltd. (Australia), a modiﬁed Rheodyne 5020 (controlled by
a small motor that can switch from the load to the inject po-
sition in 0.5 s via a switch located outside the case), and an
analog-to-digital converter described elsewhere.13 – 15 The
weight of the entire system was 2.1 kg.
Flow Injection Measurement Procedure
The portable FIP system described above was used at
ﬂow rates varying between 1.5 and 2.8 ml/min through two
channels, one for the reference electrode and the other for
the eight sensors. The ﬂow ratio between the carrier and the
reference streams of the multisensor ﬂow cell used was at
1:1. The silver and iodide standard solutions were intro-
duced manually to ﬁll the sample loop using the modiﬁed
Rheodyne (5020) injection valve described above, ﬁtted
with sample volumes ranging between 100 and 250 ml. The
carrier and reference streams used for all ﬂow injection
measurements was 100-mM sodium acetate, and in the case
of silver ion measurements 0.01-mM AgCl was added to
the 100-mM sodium acetate carrier. Prior to use, the eight
silver-based sensors were conditioned in a 100-mM sodium
acetate/0.1-mM silver iodide solution overnight.
The data acquired for each of the silver sensors were
displayed simultaneously in real-time millivolt readings on
a single computer window using the Satod© (Ver. 1.37)
data acquisition program, and were saved as text ﬁles. A
software package called IGOR PRO (AD Instruments, Syd-
ney) was used for data analysis and graphical representa-
Design of the Multisensor Flow Cell
As shown in previous studies, the appropriate design of
a miniature multisensor ﬂow cell requires the wall-embed-
ded conﬁguration,12 whereby the sensor is positioned in the
ﬂow channel and the injected sample zone ﬂows over the
surface of the electrode. This arrangement allows several
sensors to be positioned in a single ﬂow stream, whereby a
single injected sample zone can contact each of the measur-
ing electrodes while maintaining limited dispersion. See
The position of the reference electrode could be chosen
either downstream from the sensors or in a differential
arrangement, where the reference electrode is contained in
a separate ﬂow channel that merges downstream with the
multisensor channel. Studies have shown that the differen-
tial approach in potentiometric ﬂow-through cells leads to
Diagram of the portable battery-powered ﬂow injection analyzer.
FIELD ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY AND TECHNOLOGY—1996
improved precision31 and a negligible liquid junction poten-
tial at the merging point, due to rapid hydrodynamic mix-
ing.32 For this reason, the differential arrangement was em-
ployed in the multisensor ﬂow cell design presented in this
study. Employing identical concentration and composition
of the carrier and the reference solutions would also mini-
mize liquid junction potentials.32 In case identical carrier
and reference solutions can not be employed, the elec-
trolytes should be equitransferrent.33
Streaming potentials are common with potentiometric
measurements employing ﬂow-through cells.34,35 Baseline
oscillations as large as 3 mV have been observed and relate
to the revolution of the peristaltic pump.36 To minimize any
streaming potentials, low ﬂow rates and electrolyte concen-
trations $10 mM are to be used for the carrier and the ref-
erence streams.36 The multisensor ﬂow cell evaluated in this
study did not exhibit any streaming potential effects and
was the subject of studies on factors affecting the electrode
behavior of the eight sensors.
Effect of Dispersion on the Peak Heights and Peak Widths
Typical FIP responses observed for the silver electrodes
to silver and iodide ions using the eight sensor ﬂow cell are
presented in Figures 3 and 4, respectively. Figures 3(a) and
4(a) show the typical peaks observed in real time for the sil-
ver ion and iodide ion responses, respectively. Figures 3(b)
and 4(b) show the peaks for the silver ion and iodide ion re-
sponses, respectively, after baseline offsetting of each chan-
nel measured. The calibration plots for the silver and iodide
Top and side view of the multisensor ﬂow cell used in this study.
(a) Typical ﬂow injection peaks observed for the silver ion re-
sponse for each electrode in the multisensor cell displayed in real time. (b)
Flow injection peaks for the silver ion response for each electrode in the
multisensor ﬂow cell, where the baselines were offset; total ﬂow rate was
2.2 ml/min; sample injection volume was 200 ml; the ionic background of
the silver nitrate standards was 100-mM sodium acetate, and the carrier
and the reference solution was 100-mM sodium acetate with 0.01 mM
FIELD ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY AND TECHNOLOGY—1996
Comparison of the calibration plots for the multisensor response
to silver and iodide in the FIP mode.
responses observed for each electrode in the multisensor
ﬂow cell are presented in Figure 5. The silver electrode
connected to channel 7 was in closest proximity to the sam-
ple injection valve; channel 0 was the furthest away.
A general reduction in the peak height was observed as
the injected sample zone traveled from the channel 7 to the
channel 0 sensor. The observed peak heights from each of
the eight sensors for a particular injected sample zone var-
ied by up to 11%, because of the increasing dispersion of
the sample zone pumped through the ﬂow channel. The re-
producibility of the silver electrodes was determined with
eight replicate injections of a 10-mM potassium iodide
standard in a sodium acetate background in the ﬂow stream.
The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the peak heights
determined for each of the eight sensors was between 1.7%
The ﬂow injection peaks recorded simultaneously for
each electrode exhibited a response width ranging from 30
to 100 s, depending on the concentration injected and the
position of each sensor in the ﬂow channel. As shown in
Figures 3(a) and 4(a) for the response observed for silver
and iodide ions, respectively, peak widths increased from
channel 7 to channel 0 as the distance traveled by the in-
jected sample zone increased. Clearly, this suggests that
dispersion of the injected sample zone has increased as it
approaches the ﬂow channel outlet. Increasing the concen-
tration of the injected sample zone and decreasing the ﬂow
rate also resulted in increasing peak widths.
Effect of Dispersion on the Electrode Slopes
Silver Ion Response in the FIP Mode.
the injected sample zone used in this study ranged between
100 and 250 ml. The silver ion responses observed for the
eight sensor electrodes all exhibited a log-linear range of
(a) Typical ﬂow injection peaks observed for the iodide ion
response for each electrode in the multisensor cell displayed in real time.
(b) Flow injection peaks of the iodide ion response for each electrode in
the multisensor ﬂow cell, where the baselines were offset; total ﬂow rate
was 2.2 ml/min; sample injection volume was 200 ml; the ionic back-
ground of the potassium iodide standards was 100-mM sodium acetate,
and the carrier and the reference solution was 100-mM sodium acetate.
FIELD ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY AND TECHNOLOGY—1996
between 0.01 and 100 mM, using a 100-mM sodium ac-
etate carrier in the FIP mode, regardless of the sample vol-
The electrode slopes of each of the eight sensors varied
with the sample volume used, as shown in Table 1. Clearly,
the lower the sample volume used, the lower the electrode
slope observed, due to the increased dispersion of the sam-
ple zone. Using a 100ml sample volume, the RSD of the
electrode slopes of the eight measuring electrodes was
5.3%. As the sample volume increased, the RSD of the
electrode slopes ovserved for the eight sensors decreased,
as shown in Table 1. Increasing the sample volume resulted
in a similar electrode slope observed for the silver ion re-
sponses of each of the eight sensors in the FIP mode.
It was found that 200 ml was the optimum sample vol-
ume to be employed with the potentiometric ﬂow cell pre-
sented in this study. The mean electrode slope observed for
the eight silver ion responses was 57.4 6 1.0 mV per activ-
ity decade with the use of the optimum injection sample
volume of 200 ml. The RSD for the mean electrode slopes
was 1.7%. Varying the ﬂow rate between 1.5 and 2.8
ml/min had a negligible effect on the electrode slope of the
Iodide Ion Response in the FIP mode.
ion responses observed under similar conditions to the sil-
ver ion response (ﬂow rate, injected sample volume and
ionic background) were clearly sub-Nernstian in the FIP
mode, ranging from 36.3 and 50.9 mV per activity decade
in the FIP mode with the use of the optimum sample vol-
ume of 200 ml, as shown in Table 2. A log-linear range of
between 0.01 and 10 mM was observed, regardless of the
The sub-Nernstian iodide responses observed for the sil-
ver electrodes are likely to be due either to the slower re-
sponse of the electrodes to iodide, the interference of the
acetate background, or the complexation processes occur-

electrode responses to silver ions in the FIP mode; total ﬂow rate was 2.2 ml/min and the background was
100-mM sodium acetate with 0.01-mM silver chloride.

electrode responses to iodide ions in the FIP mode; total ﬂow rate was 2.2 ml/min and the background was
FIELD ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY AND TECHNOLOGY—1996
ring at the surface of the silver electrodes. The latter can be
The formation of the diiodo silver(I) complex at the surface
of the metallic silver electrode would signiﬁcantly reduce
the electrode response, leading to the observed sub-Nern-
stian response. The surface of the silver electrodes had
changed to yellow when in contact with the iodide solu-
tions. After prolonged exposure to light a black appearance
was observed, which clearly suggests the formation of the
diiodo silver (I) complex at the surface of the silver elec-
The mean electrode slope observed for the eight iodide
ion responses was 47.5 6 4.4 mV per activity decade, and
the RSD was 9.3% with the use of the determined optimum
sample volume of 200 ml. Table 2 summarizes these data.
The higher RSD observed for the iodide response was
largely contributed to the electrode slope observed for
channel 0 electrode. Excluding the channel 0 response
would result in a mean electrode slope of 49.1 6 1.3 mV
per activity decade, corresponding to a RSD of 2.7%. In-
creasing the sample volume from 200 to 250 ml results in a
similar electrode response for the channel 0 electrode, and
eliminates dispersion as the sole reason for the observed
The likely reason for the irregular response observed for
the channel 0 sensor could be a combination of the follow-
ing: (i) the interference of the acetate background and the
diiodo silver (I) complexation at the surface of this elec-
trode, (ii) a slower response to the iodide ion compared to
the other seven sensors, and (iii) the increased dispersion of
Cells-in-Series Approach for the Silver Ion and the Iodide
An alternative approach to improve the sensitivity and
detection limit of the ISEs is to employ the cells-in-series
approach26 – 30 of the eight identical sensors in this portable
FIP system. The main advantages, such as increased sensi-
tivity and improved detection limits for a single analyte,
could be available for a single anion or cation. To illustrate
this example, the eight silver ion and the eight iodide ion
responses discussed above were each added, with the use of
the IGOR PRO package, as presented in Figure 6. The de-
tection limit was also improved in cases where random
noise is a major limitation, because the increase in signal-
to-noise ratio is proportional to the square root of the num-
ber of cells in series.26,27 A ﬁve-fold improvement in the de-
tection limit was calculated for the silver ion, and the iodide
ion responses were 0.045 and 0.004 mM, respectively.
These aspects would be invaluable for environmental moni-
toring at remote site locations, where improved sensitivity
over a single cell measurement may offer the above advan-
Conclusions and Future Applications
There are numerous applications that could be utilized
with the multisensor ﬂow cell used in the portable ﬂow in-
jection system for remote site monitoring. The design of the
analog-to-digital converter employed in this portable sys-
tem would allow liquid polymer membranes selective to
different analytes to be applied to each silver sensor sub-
strate whereby liquid membranes with .10-MV resistance
could be used in the ﬂow injection mode. Chemometric
methods previously reported6 – 8,10 would be necessary to
process the data to improve the accuracy of the different ion
sensors used in the eight-sensor ﬂow cell. However, the
main obstacle to be overcome with this approach would be
the choice of the carrier solution to be used for FIP mea-
surements, so that it will not intrude on the sensitivity and
selectivity of certain liquid-membrane-based sensors.
The multisensor ﬂow cell used in the portable ﬂow in-
jection system provides peak heights of high precision and
reproducible electrode slopes for the response to silver and,
to a certain extent, iodide ions. The optimum injection vol-
ume determined for this multisensor ﬂow cell was 200 ml.
The possibility of either applying eight different sensors or
employing the cells-in-series approach makes this portable
FIP system a highly attractive analytical instrument for re-
The cells-in-series approach applied to the silver ion and the io-
dide ion responses in the FIP mode, showing the improved peak height
